cards.title: Google DeepMind Releases Gemini 3.6 Flash, 3.5 Flash-Lite, and Cyber-Specialized 3.5 Flash
cards.why_it_matters: These are production-grade flagship model releases from Google DeepMind targeting agentic workflows — directly relevant to building and deploying AI systems at scale, with concrete benchmark and cost figures.
cards.summary: Gemini 3.6 Flash improves on 3.5 Flash with 17% fewer output tokens (Artificial Analysis Index), better coding (DeepSWE: 49% vs. 37%), ML research (MLE Bench: 63.9% vs. 49.7%), and computer use (OSWorld-Verified: 83.0% vs. 78.4%), priced at $1.50/$7.50 per 1M input/output tokens. 3.5 Flash-Lite hits 350 output tokens/second. A specialized cyber model paired with CodeMender agent targets security workflows. Gemini 4 pre-training has begun.

cards.title: Survey: Simulation Engines for Physical AI and Robotics Training
cards.why_it_matters: Physical AI and robot learning are increasingly dependent on simulation infrastructure — this overview maps the landscape of engines, use cases, and the three-computer paradigm that frames modern training pipelines.
cards.summary: A detailed overview of why simulation has become central to physical AI development, covering GPU-parallelized data generation, synthetic perception datasets, RL policy training, and adversarial benchmarking. Introduces a three-computer paradigm (training cluster, simulation workstation, on-robot edge device). Covers popular engines including Isaac Sim/Lab, MuJoCo, PyBullet, Drake, and Genesis, categorized by robot domain and acceleration profile.

cards.title: GEAR: RL Reward Shaping to Cure Repetitive Copying in Long-Context Reasoning
cards.why_it_matters: Repetitive copying is a concrete, underdiagnosed failure mode in long-context reasoning models that degrades performance — this paper diagnoses it and proposes a targeted RL fix with measured gains.
cards.summary: Frontier LLMs trained with chain-of-thought reasoning copy large swaths of input text into reasoning traces rather than reasoning productively, a problem that worsens with context length. GEAR (Grounding Evidence-Aware Reward) augments standard accuracy-based RL rewards with a grounding reward for overlap with key evidence and a penalty for distractor overlap. Validated across model scales and benchmarks, yielding up to +4.6 average points over standard RL, with larger gains at longer contexts.

cards.title: Appearance Pointers: Modality-Agnostic Regional Control for Diffusion Transformers
cards.why_it_matters: Precise spatial and appearance control in DiTs is an open research problem; this paper introduces a lightweight, training-free-base-model approach that matches or beats modality-specific SOTA.
cards.summary: Appearance pointers are compact tokens produced by a region correspondence network and refined via spatial aggregation, guiding Diffusion Transformers to apply text or image appearance cues at user-specified mask regions. The approach is modality-agnostic, requires no retraining of the base DiT, and handles multiple regional descriptions without significantly increasing token load. Reaches or surpasses modality-specific SOTA across evaluated metrics.

cards.title: CodeRescue: Conformal Risk Control for Budget-Aware Recovery Routing in Coding Agents
cards.why_it_matters: Practical coding agent deployment requires principled cost-quality tradeoffs beyond simple model cascades — this paper formalizes post-failure recovery routing with statistical cost guarantees.
cards.summary: CodeRescue frames post-failure decisions in coding agents as a routing problem: when to retry with a cheap model using execution feedback vs. escalate to a stronger model. A supervised router trained on execution rollouts is paired with a Conformal Risk Control (CRC) layer that calibrates cost-penalty thresholds at deployment time without retraining. On held-out failures from five benchmarks, one CRC-calibrated point exceeds always-escalate solve rate at 35% of its mean recovery cost.

cards.title: Tutorial Survey: Design Patterns and Failure Modes for Production LLM Agents
cards.why_it_matters: As agentic systems move from benchmarks to production, understanding deployment-validated design patterns and failure mitigations is directly actionable for AI engineers building real systems.
cards.summary: A tutorial bridging academic agent research and production deployment, covering reasoning/planning advances, multi-agent coordination, and evaluation methodology. Uses applied case studies in pharmaceutical discovery and financial systems to identify common design patterns and failure modes. Discusses concrete mitigations: verification pipelines, fallback mechanisms, and human-in-the-loop supervision. Includes evaluation checklists and deployment templates.

cards.title: 1-Lipschitz Neural Networks on Hadamard Manifolds via Busemann Gradient Flows
cards.why_it_matters: Extending robustness-promoting Lipschitz constraints to non-Euclidean geometry is a niche but technically meaningful frontier for researchers working with hyperbolic or SPD-matrix-valued data.
cards.summary: Constructs 1-Lipschitz neural network layers on Hadamard manifolds (including hyperbolic space and symmetric positive definite matrices) using Busemann functions and their gradient flows, yielding quasi-α-firmly nonexpansive layers. Demonstrated on robust classification on the Poincaré disk and masked-Wishart covariance reconstruction on the SPD manifold, outperforming Log-Euclidean and static denoising baselines.

cards.title: Fundamental Limits of K-Class Classification from O(log K) Binary Classifiers
cards.why_it_matters: Understanding the theoretical performance limits of distributed classification with binary sub-classifiers is relevant to researchers designing hierarchical or ensemble classifiers with constrained communication.
cards.summary: Studies the fundamental performance limits of building a K-class classifier from O(log K) binary hyperplane classifiers in a distributed setting. For a Gaussian model with K class centers as independent Gaussian points in ℝᵈ and Gaussian observation noise, derives explicit performance bounds across multiple decoding and dimensional regimes, validated by simulation experiments.

cards.title: Grabette: Open-Source Handheld Gripper for Low-Cost Robot Manipulation Data Collection
cards.why_it_matters: Data scarcity is the core bottleneck in robot learning — Grabette offers a concrete, open-hardware path to scalable real-world manipulation dataset collection without requiring a robot arm.
cards.summary: Grabette is an open-source handheld gripper (~490€ BOM) carrying a fisheye camera and RGBD camera to capture 6-DoF manipulation demonstrations via SLAM, without needing a robot. Paired with Gripette (~120€), a motorized gripper twin for policy execution. Integrates with LeRobot datasets and Hugging Face Hub. Directly inspired by Stanford's UMI gripper. Goal is to enable crowd-sourced, large-scale open manipulation datasets.
